Taxonomy Rules: D3: AI may not use protected healthcare professional titles or claim to provide care reserved to licensed clinicians. | H1: AI may not perform reserved clinical functions without licensed professional involvement.
Specific Implications: AI providers prohibited from offering AI systems specifically programmed to provide services that constitute professional mental/behavioral healthcare; prohibited from representing AI as capable of or qualified to provide such care. | Licensed mental/behavioral health providers may use AI only for administrative support; must independently review AI-generated outputs. Public schools may not use AI for mental-health-related functions of school counselors/psychologists/social workers. Civil penalties up to $15,000 per incident.

Policy Name/Number: SB742 – Establishes a Data Sharing and Governance Working Group within the Office of Enterprise Technology Services
Summary: Enacts a Data Sharing and Governance Working Group within the Office of Enterprise Technology Services, coordinated by the Chief Data Officer, to improve inter‑agency data sharing. The group reviews current capabilities, identifies barriers, aligns with statewide strategies, and submits reports with actionable recommendations.
Healthcare Implications: Healthcare relevance is indirect. Better cross-agency data governance can support future analytics or AI readiness for public health and Medicaid, but the working group does not directly regulate AI systems or impose health-AI obligations.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: None
Specific Implications: None

Policy Name/Number: HB820 – Health Insurance – Utilization Review - Use of Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Regulates carriers’, PBMs’, and private review agents’ use of artificial intelligence, algorithms, or other software tools in utilization review. Requires determinations to account for an enrollee’s medical history and clinical circumstances; bars sole reliance on population‑level datasets; maintains clinician oversight; and sets documentation and appeal safeguards.
Healthcare Implications: Directly constrains automated prior authorization and medical‑necessity tools. Payers must implement guardrails, transparency, and human oversight; providers should expect clearer appeal documentation and be able to request human review. Aligns with national scrutiny of AI in utilization management and protects patients from algorithm‑only denials.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: U1: Adverse coverage/claims/UR determinations may not be made solely by AI; a qualified human must make the final adverse decision. | U2: AI must consider individual clinical context, not just group data. | U3: AI must meet fairness, consistency, and non-discrimination standards in coverage decisions. | P1: Internal procedures (impact assessments, periodic reviews, training) the deploying entity must maintain. | D2b: Insurers/payers must disclose AI use in coverage/claims decisions to plan sponsors, enrollees, or providers.
Specific Implications: AI tools may not replace healthcare provider decisionmaking; final medical-necessity decisions made by clinicians. | AI determinations must be based on individual medical history, individual clinical circumstances reported by provider, and other relevant clinical info; may not rely solely on group datasets. | AI tools must be applied equitably; must not discriminate. | Quarterly review of AI performance, use, and outcomes. | Documentation/disclosure requirements for AI use in UR. Applies to carriers, PBMs, and private review agents.

Policy Name/Number: HB2175 – Prior Authorization; Claims
Summary: Narrows prior-authorization and claim-review requirements by requiring appropriate medical review for adverse determinations involving medical judgment. The enacted version does not contain the earlier explicit AI-prohibition language or classify AI-only denials as unprofessional conduct.
Healthcare Implications: AI relevance is indirect: the law reinforces human medical-review expectations for adverse utilization-management decisions, but it does not explicitly regulate AI or automated decision systems in its enacted form.
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: None
Specific Implications: None

Policy Name/Number: GBL Article 47 / A3008 – Algorithmic Pricing & Artificial Intelligence Companions
Summary: Operative law under New York General Business Law Article 47, enacted through the 2025 state budget, requiring disclosure for personalized algorithmic pricing and establishing safeguards for AI companions, including crisis-response protocols and clear notices that users are interacting with AI.
Healthcare Implications: Direct implications for mental health and digital wellness tools that function as AI companions: systems must detect suicidal ideation/self-harm cues and route users to crisis services, which shapes how mental-health and wellness apps marketed in NY design, monitor, and govern AI companion features.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Transparency & Governance; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: S1: Crisis-detection and response protocols for sustained/companion AI. | D1: AI systems must disclose their non-human nature when interacting directly with users.
Specific Implications: AI companion operators must implement protocol to detect suicidal ideation, self-harm, and physical/financial harm to others; must refer users to crisis service providers. | Notification at start of every AI companion interaction and at least every 3 hours during continuing interactions; must state that user is interacting with AI and not a human, and that it is unable to feel human emotion. Civil penalties up to $15,000 per day; AG enforcement.

Policy Name/Number: HB178 – Limit Government Use of AI Systems
Summary: Restricts state/local government uses of AI, prohibiting cognitive behavioral manipulation, discriminatory classification, and broad public‑space surveillance (with narrow exceptions). Requires disclosure when agencies use AI in public‑facing tools or publish unreviewed AI content, and mandates human review for AI outputs that affect rights or duties.
Healthcare Implications: Public‑health and human services must disclose AI use in portals/chatbots and ensure trained human review before AI‑assisted decisions affect benefits, licensing, or enforcement. AI cannot be the sole basis for determinations impacting individuals. Limits on public‑space surveillance may curb live‑video analytics in health/public‑safety contexts.
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: H2: Consequential AI-driven decisions affecting individuals' rights, benefits, or welfare require human review before taking effect. | D1: AI systems must disclose their non-human nature when interacting directly with users.
Specific Implications: AI cannot be sole basis for determinations affecting rights or duties; trained human in responsible position must review/modify/reject AI recommendations affecting rights. | Disclosure when AI is used in public-facing tools or when publishing AI-generated content. Prohibits state/local government AI use for cognitive behavioral manipulation, discriminatory classification, and widespread public-space surveillance.

Policy Name/Number: SB2280 – Prior Authorization Reform Act
Summary: Prior-authorization reform requiring qualified clinical review, appeal procedures, published criteria, and reporting. The bill does not explicitly mention artificial intelligence, automated decision systems, or algorithms.
Healthcare Implications: AI relevance is implicit and indirect. The law may constrain fully automated adverse prior-authorization workflows by requiring qualified human review, but it should not be coded as an explicit AI governance law.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: None
Specific Implications: None

Policy Name/Number: HB956 – Consumer Protection – Workgroup on Artificial Intelligence Implementation
Summary: Establishes the Workgroup on Artificial Intelligence Implementation within the Office of the Attorney General to study AI’s consumer‑protection implications, current private‑sector uses, enforcement authority, and impacts on government‑benefits determinations, and to report recommendations to the General Assembly by July 1, 2026.
Healthcare Implications: Provides a formal venue for AI policy recommendations that can shape future health‑sector rules on transparency, bias mitigation, and consumer protections. Health systems, payers, and vendors may be called to provide input and should monitor the workgroup’s outputs for compliance impacts.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors
Taxonomy Rules: G1: Advisory study commission or task force.
Specific Implications: Establishes Workgroup on AI Implementation within AG's office to study consumer-protection implications of AI; report due July 1, 2026.

Policy Name/Number: HJ4 – Interim Study of Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Directs a legislative interim study of AI, including a survey of Montana, other state, and federal laws; exploration of an AI code of ethics and potential bill of rights; and analysis of transparency/disclosure requirements and civil/criminal liability for AI developers and deployers.
Healthcare Implications: Study scope includes impacts on state agencies and residents–touching health data, bias, access, and patient safety. Findings could shape future MT healthcare‑specific AI policy and procurement standards; providers, patient advocates, and vendors may wish to participate.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: G3: Directs a one-time agency study without rulemaking authority.
Specific Implications: Directs a legislative interim study of AI, including survey of laws, AI code of ethics, bill of rights, and liability analysis.

Policy Name/Number: HB1876 – Regarding the Ownership of Model Training and Content Generated by a Generative Artificial Intelligence Tool
Summary: Clarifies ownership of content generated by generative AI tools and certain model-training outputs, including work-for-hire exceptions and contractual allocation of rights.
Healthcare Implications: AI relevance is general IP/commercial law rather than health-AI governance. Health systems, universities, and vendors may consider the statute when allocating ownership of AI-assisted outputs, but it does not regulate healthcare AI safety, transparency, privacy, or clinical use.
Stakeholder Tags: Developers & Vendors; Providers & Health Systems
Taxonomy Rules: None
Specific Implications: None

Policy Name/Number: HB2342 – Blockchain Technology; Regulation; Computational power
Summary: Preempts local governments from restricting AI development, deployment, computational resources, data centers, or model training activities authorized by state or federal law.
Healthcare Implications: AI relevance is infrastructure and local-preemption oriented, not direct health-AI governance. It may affect where AI developers or health-tech vendors locate compute resources, but it does not create healthcare AI safety, disclosure, clinical, or payer obligations.
Stakeholder Tags: Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: None
Specific Implications: None

Policy Name/Number: HB1958 – To Require Public Entities to Create a Policy Concerning the Authorized Use of Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Requires Arkansas public entities (state agencies, local governments, public schools, and higher-ed) to adopt an AI/automated decision tool policy that defines authorized uses, ensures human-in-the-loop review, and documents oversight. Mandates disclosures and accountability mechanisms when AI assists administrative decisions.
Healthcare Implications: Directly impacts public health agencies, public hospitals, and public universities’ health programs by requiring formal AI acceptable-use policies with human accountability. Expect new procurement and compliance checklists (e.g., documenting model purpose, data sources, and human review). Health entities should align with National Institute of Standards and Technology AI RMF and maintain audit trails for AI-assisted determinations affecting patients or benefits.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems
Taxonomy Rules: H2: Consequential AI-driven decisions affecting individuals' rights, benefits, or welfare require human review before taking effect. | P1: Internal procedures (impact assessments, periodic reviews, training) the deploying entity must maintain. | P2: Deploying entities must publicly disclose or file with regulators detailed information about their AI systems, policies, or inventories.
Specific Implications: All public entities must adopt policy mandating that a human employee or designee always makes the final decision, regardless of AI recommendation. | Must adopt formal AI policy defining authorized uses, develop employee training program, create disciplinary procedures for violations. | AI policies must be made publicly available upon request. Covers state agencies, local governments, public schools, higher ed.

Policy Name/Number: SB212 – Right to Compute Act
Summary: Protects the right to use personal computational resources while including AI-related provisions relevant to risk management and critical-infrastructure contexts.
Healthcare Implications: Healthcare relevance is indirect. The law is not a health-AI governance statute, but its AI and compute provisions may matter for developers or infrastructure operators supporting AI systems used in health settings.
Stakeholder Tags: Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: P1: Internal procedures (impact assessments, periodic reviews, training) the deploying entity must maintain.
Specific Implications: Includes AI-related risk-management expectations in critical-infrastructure contexts. Healthcare relevance is indirect and infrastructure-oriented, not a direct clinical, payer, or patient-facing AI obligation.

Policy Name/Number: HB2313 – Prohibiting the Use of the Artificial Intelligence Platform DeepSeek and other Artificial Intelligence Platforms...
Summary: Prohibits the use of the DeepSeek platform and other AI platforms controlled by a 'country of concern' on state-owned devices and networks, and restricts genetic sequencers or operational software produced by a foreign adversary.
Healthcare Implications: State labs, public hospitals, university medical centers, and health programs must audit AI tools and laboratory equipment, block prohibited platforms, and review vendor contracts and firmware/software to ensure compliance without disrupting clinical workflows.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: I3: Foreign-adversary AI or equipment prohibited on state systems.
Specific Implications: Prohibits specified foreign-adversary technology (including DeepSeek and other AI platforms controlled by countries of concern) on state-owned devices and networks; restricts genetic sequencers or operational software produced by a foreign adversary.

Policy Name/Number: HB178 – Nursing Practice Changes
Summary: Amends the New Mexico Nursing Practice Act to clarify nursing scope and Board of Nursing powers, including authority for the Board to promulgate rules on the use of artificial intelligence in nursing.
Healthcare Implications: Creates enabling authority for future nursing AI rules, but does not itself impose substantive operational obligations on nurses, health systems, or AI vendors until the Board of Nursing acts.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government; Developers & Vendors
Taxonomy Rules: G2: Directs an agency to issue binding rules, standards, or regulatory guidance on AI.
Specific Implications: Authorizes the Board of Nursing to promulgate rules on the use of AI in nursing. Currently only an enabling provision; no substantive AI obligations until the Board acts.

Policy Name/Number: SR391 – Senate Study Committee on Artificial Intelligence and Digital Currency
Summary: Creates the Senate Study Committee on Artificial Intelligence and Digital Currency to examine policy impacts, risks, and opportunities across Georgia’s economy, including public services. The committee is tasked with hearings and recommendations for the 2025–2026 biennium.
Healthcare Implications: Healthcare testimony is expected on AI safety, workforce augmentation, and data privacy. Findings could shape subsequent bills on guardrails for hospital AI systems, insurer use of algorithms, and patient‑facing applications (e.g., symptom checkers), influencing compliance expectations for health entities.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: G1: Advisory study commission or task force.
Specific Implications: Creates Senate Study Committee on AI and Digital Currency for 2025-2026 biennium.

Policy Name/Number: SB0226 – Artificial Intelligence Consumer Protection Amendments
Summary: Creates consumer‑protection requirements around AI use, including disclosures for AI‑mediated interactions and guardrails against harmful impersonation or deception. Directs enforcement via state consumer‑protection authorities and sets penalties for violations.
Healthcare Implications: Patient‑facing bots, voice agents, and automated outreach used by health systems must clearly disclose AI involvement and avoid deceptive impersonation. Marketing, scheduling, and billing workflows using generative tools should implement policy, logging, and quality assurance to meet the new standards.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors
Taxonomy Rules: D1: AI systems must disclose their non-human nature when interacting directly with users. | D2a: Healthcare practitioners must disclose to patients when using AI in their care.
Specific Implications: Narrows UAIPA disclosure trigger: general consumer requests must now be 'clear and unambiguous'; GenAI definition narrowed to systems simulating human conversation. | Narrows regulated-occupation disclosure to 'high-risk AI interactions' only (involving collection of sensitive personal info like health/biometric data, or personalized recommendations/advice in healthcare, law, or finance). Safe harbor: no enforcement action if GenAI itself discloses non-human nature at outset and throughout interaction. Amends UT SB0149 (UAIPA); effective May 7, 2025.

Policy Name/Number: HB0452 – Artificial Intelligence Amendments
Summary: Adds targeted requirements for mental health chatbots, including clear AI disclosures, limits on selling, sharing, or using user health information and inputs for advertising, and state-filed policies describing chatbot purpose, limitations, safety protocols, and licensed-professional involvement.
Healthcare Implications: Directly affects mental-health chatbot suppliers and digital behavioral-health vendors. Covered tools need front-end AI disclosures, tighter data-use controls, advertising restrictions, and documented safety/governance policies filed with the state.
Keyword Tags: Transparency & Governance; Privacy & Data; Safety & Risk
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: D1: AI systems must disclose their non-human nature when interacting directly with users. | I4: Patient data used in AI may not be used beyond intended purpose. | P2: Deploying entities must publicly disclose or file with regulators detailed information about their AI systems, policies, or inventories.
Specific Implications: Mental health chatbots must clearly and conspicuously disclose AI nature: before user accesses features, at start of any interaction (if user hasn't accessed in 7 days), and when user asks. | Suppliers may not sell or share user IIHI or user input with third parties (limited exceptions); may not use user input for advertising. | Suppliers must develop and file detailed policies with the state outlining the chatbot's purpose, limitations, safety protocols, and involvement of licensed mental health professionals in development.

Policy Name/Number: SB0332 – Artificial Intelligence Revisions
Summary: Procedural amendments to Utah’s AI statutory framework, including extension of the Utah Artificial Intelligence Policy Act sunset and coordination of definitions with other 2025 AI legislation.
Healthcare Implications: No major new substantive healthcare AI obligation. The bill preserves and coordinates Utah’s broader AI governance framework, which can affect health-related AI actors through adjacent statutes, but this revision is primarily procedural.
Stakeholder Tags: Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: None
Specific Implications: None

Policy Name/Number: SB4 – An Act Relating to Protection of Information and Declaring an Emergency
Summary: Establishes a statewide, risk-based governance framework for AI used by Kentucky government; directs the Commonwealth Office of Technology to set and enforce standards; creates an AI Governance Committee; requires disclosure of public‑sector AI use and employee training; and adds synthetic‑media disclosure/cause-of-action provisions. Includes emergency provisions.
Healthcare Implications: State health agencies must adopt AI standards for eligibility, program integrity, surveillance, and other AI‑enabled systems, with inventories, privacy/security controls, human oversight, and disclosures where applicable. Procurements for AI tools will likely require compliance attestations.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors
Taxonomy Rules: H2: Consequential AI-driven decisions affecting individuals' rights, benefits, or welfare require human review before taking effect. | D1: AI systems must disclose their non-human nature when interacting directly with users. | P1: Internal procedures (impact assessments, periodic reviews, training) the deploying entity must maintain. | P2: Deploying entities must publicly disclose or file with regulators detailed information about their AI systems, policies, or inventories. | P4: Deploying entities must complete specified pre-deployment steps before an AI system may be used. | G2: Directs an agency to issue binding rules, standards, or regulatory guidance on AI.
Specific Implications: Human review required for consequential AI-driven decisions. | Public disclosure when AI is used in decision-making affecting citizens. | Anti-discrimination documentation; data security/privacy; employee education and training. | Centralized registry of AI systems maintained by COT. | State agencies must obtain COT approval before implementing AI systems (pre-deployment approval gate). | COT establishes AI policy standards aligned with NIST and ISO benchmarks; AI Governance Committee within COT.

Policy Name/Number: SB2426 – Artificial Intelligence Regulation (AIR) Task Force
Summary: Establishes the AIR Task Force to study the use, risks, and regulatory needs of AI across sectors in Mississippi. Membership includes representatives from state agencies, universities, and technical experts. Duties include assessing AI’s impacts on healthcare, public health data management, diagnostics, and medical decision-making, and recommending safeguards and best practices.
Healthcare Implications: Directly relevant to AI in healthcare through mandated review of AI’s role in diagnostics, patient care, and public health policy, with potential to shape state health regulations and standards.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: G1: Advisory study commission or task force.
Specific Implications: Establishes AIR Task Force to study AI across sectors; scope explicitly includes healthcare diagnostics, patient care, and public health data management.

Policy Name/Number: SB896 – Generative Artificial Intelligence Accountability Act
Summary: Requires California state agencies to report on and disclose specified uses of generative AI, directs updates to statewide GenAI reporting, requires analysis of GenAI risks to critical infrastructure, and embeds portions of the Governor’s AI executive order into statute.
Healthcare Implications: Healthcare implications are indirect but meaningful for state health agencies and vendors working with public-facing or state-operated GenAI systems. The law creates binding public-sector reporting and disclosure expectations that can shape health-agency procurement, provenance, risk assessment, and escalation practices.
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Developers & Vendors; Patients & Public; Providers & Health Systems
Taxonomy Rules: P2: Deploying entities must publicly disclose or file with regulators detailed information about their AI systems, policies, or inventories. | G3: Directs a one-time agency study without rulemaking authority.
Specific Implications: State agencies must conduct and report on AI-related studies including inventory of current AI use, risk assessment, and workforce impact analysis. Imposes binding state-agency disclosure mandate. | Directs specified state agencies to study and report on AI use, risks, and opportunities.

Policy Name/Number: SB1120 – Physicians Make Decisions Act
Summary: Regulates payors’ use of AI and automated decision tools in utilization management by requiring qualified clinical decision-making, individual clinical-context review, fairness and non-discrimination, auditability, written AI oversight policies, periodic review, limits on secondary patient-data use, and protections against AI-related harm.
Healthcare Implications: One of the strongest payer-facing health-AI laws. Health plans and insurers must keep human clinical oversight over medical-necessity decisions, prevent discriminatory or group-data-only determinations, make AI policies available, support regulator audits, limit patient-data reuse, and avoid AI practices that harm enrollees.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Equity & Bias; Privacy & Data
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: U1: Adverse coverage/claims/UR determinations may not be made solely by AI; a qualified human must make the final adverse decision. | U2: AI must consider individual clinical context, not just group data. | U3: AI must meet fairness, consistency, and non-discrimination standards in coverage decisions. | U4: Regulator oversight of insurer AI use (certification, reporting, audit authority). | P1: Internal procedures (impact assessments, periodic reviews, training) the deploying entity must maintain. | D2b: Insurers/payers must disclose AI use in coverage/claims decisions to plan sponsors, enrollees, or providers. | I4: Patient data used in AI may not be used beyond intended purpose.
Specific Implications: AI may not supplant healthcare provider decisionmaking; medical-necessity determinations made only by licensed physician or licensed healthcare professional competent to evaluate specific clinical issues. | AI determinations must be based on enrollee's medical history, individual clinical circumstances, other relevant medical records; may not rely solely on group dataset. | AI must not discriminate directly or indirectly against enrollees; must be fairly and equitably applied including in accordance with HHS regulations and guidance. | AI must be open to inspection for audit or compliance reviews by the department; director includes review of compliance as part of periodic medical surveys. | AI performance, use, and outcomes periodically reviewed and revised to maximize accuracy and reliability. | Disclosures pertaining to use and oversight of AI must be contained in written policies and procedures. | Patient data used in AI may not be used beyond intended and stated purpose. AI must not directly or indirectly cause harm to enrollee. Willful violation by HCSP is a crime.

Policy Name/Number: AB3030 – Health Care Services: Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Requires health facilities, clinics, and physician/group practices that use generative AI to generate written or verbal patient communications about clinical information to include both (1) a disclaimer indicating AI was used and (2) clear instructions for how the patient can reach a human clinician/staff. Communications reviewed by a licensed provider are exempt; violations by physicians are subject to Medical Board/Osteopathic Board jurisdiction.
Healthcare Implications: Directly impacts patient‑facing chatbots, portal messages, automated call scripts, and discharge instructions, mandating transparency and human‑escalation pathways. Drives changes to vendor contracts, quality assurance, and risk management for generative AI in care delivery. Supports patient comprehension and informed consent by clarifying when AI assists in clinical communications.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public
Taxonomy Rules: D2a: Healthcare practitioners must disclose to patients when using AI in their care.
Specific Implications: Health facilities, clinics, physician's offices, or group practices using GenAI to generate written or verbal patient communications pertaining to patient clinical information must include: (1) disclaimer that communication was generated by GenAI with format-specific requirements (prominently at start of letters/emails, throughout chat, verbally at start/end of audio, displayed throughout video); and (2) clear instructions on how patient may contact a human healthcare provider. Exempt if communication is reviewed by a licensed provider. Physician violations subject to Medical Board jurisdiction.

Policy Name/Number: AB2885 – Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Creates a uniform definition of “artificial intelligence” across multiple California codes and directs the Government Operations Agency and Department of Technology to coordinate planning around deepfakes and AI risks. Updates references to high‑risk automated decision systems inventory and certain local reporting about automation’s job impacts.
Healthcare Implications: Provides a common statutory definition agencies (including health and human services) can rely on when crafting or applying AI rules, reducing ambiguity for providers, payers, and health‑IT vendors. Supports statewide risk assessment of AI/Automated Decision System used by government programs that interact with healthcare (e.g., eligibility, fraud, benefits). Sets groundwork for future health‑specific AI guidance to align to a shared baseline.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: G2: Directs an agency to issue binding rules, standards, or regulatory guidance on AI.
Specific Implications: Creates uniform statutory definition of AI across California codes; directs Government Operations Agency and Department of Technology to coordinate planning on AI risks.

Policy Name/Number: AB1282 – Mental Health: Impacts of Social Media
Summary: Requires the Department of Public Health, in consultation with the Mental Health Services Oversight and Accountability Commission, to deliver by Dec. 31, 2026 a statewide strategy to understand, communicate, and mitigate youth mental‑health risks associated with social media. Specifies analyses, outreach, and recommendations.
Healthcare Implications: Healthcare relevance is indirect. The measure concerns youth mental-health impacts of social media and may inform digital-health or platform-policy discussions, but it is not an AI regulation and does not create AI-specific healthcare obligations.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public
Taxonomy Rules: None
Specific Implications: None

Policy Name/Number: SB1223 – Consumer Privacy: Sensitive Personal Information: Neural Data
Summary: Defines “neural data” (e.g., EEG/fMRI/BCI signals, eye‑tracking, neural recordings) as Sensitive Personal Information under the California Consumer Privacy Act. Extends CCPA rights and duties to neural data–right to know/limit/opt‑out, purpose limitation, and reasonable security–and prohibits selling or sharing neural data without opt‑in consent except as permitted by law.
Healthcare Implications: Neurotechnology, mental‑health apps, and medical devices that collect brain‑signal or neuro‑adjacent data must treat it as highly protected SPI, tightening consent and limiting secondary uses (e.g., AI training) outside Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act. Providers and vendors integrating BCIs or neuro‑monitoring need new data‑governance controls and contractual terms for third‑party analytics. Raises compliance expectations for de‑identification and security when neural data are combined with other patient information.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems
Taxonomy Rules: I4: Patient data used in AI may not be used beyond intended purpose.
Specific Implications: Defines 'neural data' (EEG, fMRI, BCI signals, eye-tracking, neural recordings) as Sensitive Personal Information under CCPA; extends CCPA rights to neural data (right to know, limit, opt-out, purpose limitation, reasonable security); prohibits selling or sharing neural data without opt-in consent. Healthcare relevance: neurotechnology and mental health apps collecting brain-signal data must treat it as highly protected SPI.

Policy Name/Number: AB2013 – Generative Artificial Intelligence: Training Data Transparency
Summary: Imposes training‑data documentation and transparency obligations on certain developers of generative AI models, including disclosure of data sources, copyrighted content, personal‑data handling, and known risks. Establishes posting and retention requirements and deadlines for frontier models.
Healthcare Implications: Health‑AI vendors (clinical documentation, coding, patient engagement) must provide clearer provenance for model training data, enabling health systems to evaluate privacy, consent, and bias risks before deployment. Supports safer adoption of gen‑AI in clinical and administrative workflows by improving auditability and due diligence for PHI‑adjacent datasets.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: P2: Deploying entities must publicly disclose or file with regulators detailed information about their AI systems, policies, or inventories.
Specific Implications: Developers of GenAI models must document and disclose training data sources, copyrighted content, personal-data handling, and known risks; posting and retention requirements. Extends P2 from deployers to developers with focus on training data transparency. Healthcare relevance: health-AI vendors must provide clearer provenance for model training data.

Policy Name/Number: SB942 – California AI Transparency Act
Summary: Requires large generative-AI providers to support content provenance and disclosure for AI-generated image, video, and audio content, including detection tools and manifest/latent disclosures.
Healthcare Implications: Healthcare impact is indirect. The law primarily affects upstream GenAI providers and third-party licensees, not healthcare providers directly; health organizations may encounter compliance mainly through vendor supply chains or AI-generated media workflows.
Stakeholder Tags: Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government; Patients & Public
Taxonomy Rules: D1: AI systems must disclose their non-human nature when interacting directly with users.
Specific Implications: Covered providers (GenAI providers with >1M monthly users in CA) must offer free AI detection tool, allow users to add visible 'manifest disclosure' labeling content as AI-generated, embed 'latent disclosure' (provenance metadata) in AI-generated image/video/audio; third-party licensees must preserve disclosures. Healthcare relevance is indirect — applies to upstream GenAI providers, not healthcare actors directly.

Policy Name/Number: S3432 – Next New Jersey Program Act
Summary: Establishes the Next New Jersey Program within the New Jersey Economic Development Authority to support AI investment through transferable tax credits and project agreements for eligible AI businesses and large-scale AI data centers.
Healthcare Implications: Healthcare relevance is economic-development oriented and indirect. Health AI startups, life-science companies, or academic medical centers may benefit from AI investment incentives, but the law does not impose AI governance, clinical safety, privacy, or transparency obligations.
Stakeholder Tags: Developers & Vendors; Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: None
Specific Implications: None

Policy Name/Number: HB2472 – Ins-Adverse Determination
Summary: Amends Illinois utilization review requirements so that when a health plan or other utilization review program uses algorithmic automated processes in medical-necessity review, only a clinical peer may issue adverse determinations, and any such systems must rely on objective, evidence-based criteria consistent with URAC or National Committee for Quality Assurance accreditation.
Healthcare Implications: Directly regulates use of AI-enabled utilization review in health insurance, limiting fully automated adverse decisions and reinforcing human clinical oversight and evidence-based criteria for coverage decisions affecting patient access to care.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: U1: Adverse coverage/claims/UR determinations may not be made solely by AI; a qualified human must make the final adverse decision. | U3: AI must meet fairness, consistency, and non-discrimination standards in coverage decisions.
Specific Implications: Even when algorithmic automated processes are used in UR for medical necessity, only a clinical peer may make adverse determinations or process appeals. | UR programs using algorithmic automated processes must use objective, evidence-based criteria compliant with URAC or NCQA accreditation standards.

Policy Name/Number: HB333 – An Act to Amend Title 29 of the Delaware Code Relating to the Artificial Intelligence Commission
Summary: Establishes the Delaware Artificial Intelligence Commission to advise the Governor and General Assembly on AI risks, opportunities, and governance. The Commission surveys AI use in state agencies, engages stakeholders, and recommends standards, procurement practices, and transparency measures; initial appointments and deliverables were set in the 2024 law.
Healthcare Implications: Health and human services agencies will receive guidance on responsible AI adoption, including consent, explainability, and bias mitigation for algorithms used in benefits eligibility, utilization management, and clinical decision support. The Commission provides a forum to align health‑sector practices with statewide norms and to surface sector‑specific safeguards.
Stakeholder Tags: Developers & Vendors
Taxonomy Rules: G1: Advisory study commission or task force.
Specific Implications: Establishes Delaware AI Commission to advise Governor and General Assembly on AI risks, opportunities, and governance; surveys agency AI use; recommends standards and procurement practices.

Policy Name/Number: HB1688 – Use of AI by State Agencies
Summary: Creates RSA 5‑D governing AI use by state agencies: requires transparency when the public interacts with AI, mandates human review for significant decisions, and establishes guardrails on biometric identification/surveillance. Includes definitions and agency governance duties.
Healthcare Implications: Public‑sector health programs using AI (e.g., eligibility chatbots, triage, fraud analytics) must disclose AI use and add human review for impactful determinations. Restrictions on real‑time biometric ID limit live facial recognition uses in health/public safety contexts. Agencies must conduct risk assessments and implement governance when procuring AI health tools.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors
Taxonomy Rules: H2: Consequential AI-driven decisions affecting individuals' rights, benefits, or welfare require human review before taking effect. | D1: AI systems must disclose their non-human nature when interacting directly with users. | E1: AI systems must be evaluated for discriminatory or biased outcomes; entities must take specified action if discrimination is found. | P2: Deploying entities must publicly disclose or file with regulators detailed information about their AI systems, policies, or inventories.
Specific Implications: Irreversible AI-generated recommendations or decisions affecting rights, biometric ID, critical infrastructure, law enforcement, or legal interpretation must be reviewed by a trained human aware of the AI's limitations. | AI interactions with users must be disclosed; generative AI material not human-reviewed must include AI disclosure. | Prohibits AI classification of persons resulting in unlawful discrimination. | Within 9 months, all state agencies must review AI use and report to DoIT; DoIT publishes annual report on agency AI systems on its website.

Policy Name/Number: HR170 – Directing the Joint State Government Commission to Establish an Advisory Committee...
Summary: Directs the Pennsylvania Joint State Government Commission to establish an advisory committee to study artificial intelligence and make recommendations on AI governance, oversight, and responsible public-sector use.
Healthcare Implications: May inform Pennsylvania state-agency AI governance, including agencies or programs connected to Medicaid, public health, licensing, and benefits administration. It does not impose direct healthcare AI obligations, but it could shape future standards for transparency, accountability, and human oversight in public-sector health programs.
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors
Taxonomy Rules: G1: Advisory study commission or task force.
Specific Implications: Directs Joint State Government Commission to establish AI advisory committee implementing Governor's EO on AI governance.

Policy Name/Number: SB818 – Artificial Intelligence Governance Act of 2024
Summary: Requires each unit of Maryland state government to inventory AI systems; conduct impact assessments; and, under the Department of Information Technology, adopt policies and procedures governing the development, procurement, deployment, use, and assessment of AI.
Healthcare Implications: Applies to state health entities (e.g., MDH, Medicaid) that operate AI‑enabled systems in eligibility, program integrity, and public‑health analytics. Expect standardized inventories, risk assessments, and governance processes that vendors must satisfy when contracting with the state.
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: P2: Deploying entities must publicly disclose or file with regulators detailed information about their AI systems, policies, or inventories. | G2: Directs an agency to issue binding rules, standards, or regulatory guidance on AI. | G1: Advisory study commission or task force.
Specific Implications: Each unit of state government must conduct inventories and impact assessments of high-risk AI systems. | DoIT directed to adopt policies and procedures for development, procurement, deployment, use, and assessment of state AI systems. | Establishes Governor's AI Subcabinet; develops roadmap; submits reports to Governor and General Assembly.

Policy Name/Number: SB487 – JCOTS study on AI use by public bodies
Summary: Directs the Joint Commission on Technology and Science to examine and provide recommendations on the use of artificial intelligence by public bodies; authorizes study, reporting, and coordination with agencies.
Healthcare Implications: Virginia health agencies and public hospitals fall within the scope of JCOTS’ examination. Expect future standards on transparency, inventories, and human oversight for AI used in benefits, licensing, and public‑health programs.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors
Taxonomy Rules: G3: Directs a one-time agency study without rulemaking authority.
Specific Implications: Directs JCOTS to examine and recommend on AI use by public bodies; authorizes study, reporting, and coordination with agencies.

Policy Name/Number: SB7018 – Health Care Innovation Council and Revolving Loan Program
Summary: Creates the Health Care Innovation Council within the Department of Health and establishes a revolving loan program to scale promising health innovations statewide. The Council advises on priorities and oversees annual reporting.
Healthcare Implications: AI‑enabled solutions (clinical decision support, imaging triage, virtual care, revenue cycle automation) can qualify for technical assistance and financing, accelerating adoption by hospitals and community providers. Expect pilots to require outcome tracking, privacy/security compliance, and equitable access considerations to receive support.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors
Taxonomy Rules: G1: Advisory study commission or task force.
Specific Implications: Creates Health Care Innovation Council within DOH; establishes revolving loan program for health innovations. Not AI-specific but health-innovation focused.

Policy Name/Number: SB5838 – Establishing an Artificial Intelligence Task Force
Summary: Creates a statewide Artificial Intelligence Task Force to study AI uses and risks across sectors; requires recommendations on governance, transparency, and workforce impacts.
Healthcare Implications: Health stakeholders are included among sectors to be consulted, with implications for standards around clinical decision support, patient communications, and equity in AI deployment in Washington.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors
Taxonomy Rules: G1: Advisory study commission or task force.
Specific Implications: Creates statewide AI Task Force to study AI uses and risks; requires recommendations on governance, transparency, and workforce impacts.

Policy Name/Number: SB0149 – Artificial Intelligence Amendments
Summary: Updates Utah’s AI statutes with definitions and governance features, including disclosures for certain AI‑generated content and standards for state use. The measure refines responsibilities for agencies and clarifies prohibited practices, with rulemaking delegated to appropriate state offices.
Healthcare Implications: State health programs and contractors deploying conversational agents or generative outputs should ensure user disclosures and human oversight. Procurements touching Medicaid, licensing, or public‑health analytics will need to align with the clarified statutory standards.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors
Taxonomy Rules: D1: AI systems must disclose their non-human nature when interacting directly with users. | D2a: Healthcare practitioners must disclose to patients when using AI in their care. | G2: Directs an agency to issue binding rules, standards, or regulatory guidance on AI.
Specific Implications: GenAI in non-regulated consumer transaction must disclose AI nature when consumer asks (narrowed in 2025 to 'clear and unambiguous request'). | Regulated occupations (including healthcare professions) must prominently disclose GenAI use at outset of interaction (narrowed in 2025 by SB0226 to 'high-risk AI interactions' only). | Office of AI Policy with rulemaking authority; AI Learning Laboratory Program (regulatory sandbox).

Policy Name/Number: SB0150 – Artificial Intelligence and Cybersecurity
Summary: Creates the Indiana Artificial Intelligence Task Force to study and assess the use of AI by state agencies and to recommend policies on technology resources and cybersecurity. Authorizes public entities to adopt technology and cybersecurity policies, directs inventories of AI technologies in use, and clarifies state software licensing oversight.
Healthcare Implications: Indiana’s health agencies and state-run health programs must inventory and evaluate AI tools (e.g., triage chatbots, predictive analytics, fraud detection) and align with statewide cybersecurity requirements. Task Force guidance can shape procurement criteria for Electronic Health Record add‑ons, decision‑support software, and vendor risk management.
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: G1: Advisory study commission or task force. | P2: Deploying entities must publicly disclose or file with regulators detailed information about their AI systems, policies, or inventories.
Specific Implications: Creates Indiana AI Task Force to study and assess AI use by state agencies. | Directs inventories of AI technologies in use by public entities. One of few task-force bills with an actual operative inventory provision.

Policy Name/Number: HB5690 – Creating a West Virginia Task Force on Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Creates the West Virginia Task Force on Artificial Intelligence within the Office of Technology; sets membership, duties (including a report), and a termination date.
Healthcare Implications: Task force scope includes examining AI’s impacts on public services and the workforce, relevant to public hospitals and health programs. Expect recommendations that influence procurement, transparency, and risk management for AI used in state‑connected health systems.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors
Taxonomy Rules: G1: Advisory study commission or task force.
Specific Implications: Creates WV Task Force on AI within Office of Technology; sets membership, duties, reporting, and termination date.

Policy Name/Number: HR3 – Creating a Select Committee on Artificial Intelligence
Summary: House Resolution creating a Select Committee on Artificial Intelligence for the remainder of the 86th Legislature to study AI issues and recommend action to the Speaker.
Healthcare Implications: Signals legislative attention to AI impacts across sectors, including healthcare delivery and patient data. Provides a venue for stakeholder input that may shape future AI guardrails affecting providers and payers.
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Providers & Health Systems; Payers & Purchasers
Taxonomy Rules: G1: Advisory study commission or task force.
Specific Implications: Creates Select Committee on AI for the 86th Legislature to study AI issues and recommend action.

Policy Name/Number: AB302 – Department of Technology: High-Risk Automated Decision Systems: Inventory
Summary: Directs the California Department of Technology to conduct and publish an inventory of high‑risk automated decision systems used, developed, or procured by state agencies; defines ADS and requires risk‑management policies and ongoing reporting.
Healthcare Implications: Covers Automated Decision System deployed by state health entities (e.g., Medi‑Cal eligibility, program integrity, triage), increasing transparency into government AI that affects access to care and benefits. Encourages bias testing and accountability measures that health providers and advocacy groups can monitor. May influence procurement standards for AI tools interfacing with public health and human services programs.
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: P2: Deploying entities must publicly disclose or file with regulators detailed information about their AI systems, policies, or inventories.
Specific Implications: Department of Technology must conduct comprehensive inventory of all high-risk ADM systems used/proposed by state agencies (decisions, alternatives, efficacy research, data categories, mitigation measures); annual report to Legislature. 'High-risk' defined to include healthcare decisions.

Policy Name/Number: SB01103 – An Act Concerning Artificial Intelligence, Automated Decision-Making and Personal Data Privacy
Summary: Establishes an Office of Artificial Intelligence; requires annual inventories of AI systems used by executive and judicial branches; mandates policies and procedures for state agency AI development, procurement, use, and assessments; and amends the state privacy law (including teen targeted‑ads limits).
Healthcare Implications: Covers AI governance for state health agencies (e.g., DSS, DPH), including risk assessment, transparency, and bias mitigation for automated systems that can affect benefits eligibility, licensing, or other health‑related decisions. A reference point for hospital compliance teams engaging with state systems.
Keyword Tags: Transparency & Governance; Privacy & Data; Equity & Bias; Safety & Risk
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Providers & Health Systems
Taxonomy Rules: P2: Deploying entities must publicly disclose or file with regulators detailed information about their AI systems, policies, or inventories. | E1: AI systems must be evaluated for discriminatory or biased outcomes; entities must take specified action if discrimination is found. | G2: Directs an agency to issue binding rules, standards, or regulatory guidance on AI. | P4: Deploying entities must complete specified pre-deployment steps before an AI system may be used. | G1: Advisory study commission or task force.
Specific Implications: DAS conducts annual inventory of all state agency AI systems (name, vendor, capabilities, decision involvement, impact-assessment status); inventory publicly accessible on state open data portal. | Ongoing assessments specifically for unlawful discrimination. | Establishes Office of AI; AI officer develops/adopts automated systems procedures. | Agencies must give 60-day advance notice to Office of AI before developing or acquiring new AI system. | Task force to develop AI Bill of Rights.

Policy Name/Number: SCR179 – Urging Congress To Begin A Discussion Considering The Benefits And Risks Of Artificial Intelligence Technologies
Summary: Companion concurrent resolution urging Congress to weigh the benefits and risks of AI technologies and to develop policy responses. Highlights the rapid evolution of AI capabilities and the importance of oversight.
Healthcare Implications: Frames federal engagement on AI policy areas – privacy, bias, safety – that directly affect health systems’ use of AI tools. Provides context for state action and coordination with U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, U.S. Food and Drug Administration, Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology, and National Institute of Standards and Technology guidance.
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: G4: Resolution or memorial expressing legislative position on AI.
Specific Implications: Resolution urging Congress to weigh benefits and risks of AI technologies and develop policy responses.

Policy Name/Number: HB203 – Consumer Protection in Eye Care Act (Tele-Optometry Assessment Mechanisms)
Summary: Revises restrictions on the sale and dispensing of contact lenses and sets requirements for remote “assessment mechanisms,” including physician involvement, patient identity and health verification, recordkeeping, and liability coverage.
Healthcare Implications: Regulates tele-optometry and online contact lens services that rely on automated or algorithmic assessment tools, shaping how such tools can be used for vision-related care and safeguarding clinical oversight and patient safety.
Keyword Tags: Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: H1: AI may not perform reserved clinical functions without licensed professional involvement.
Specific Implications: Sets requirements for remote 'assessment mechanisms' in eye care, including physician involvement, patient identity and health verification, recordkeeping, and liability coverage. Constrains automated vision assessment tools.

Policy Name/Number: H6654 – Consumer Protection in Eye Care Act (Assessment Mechanisms)
Summary: Establishes standards and enforcement protocols for eye care services, including definitions and requirements for remote or automated “assessment mechanisms” used to generate prescriptions or recommendations.
Healthcare Implications: Constrains how telehealth and automated eye assessment technologies can be used in Rhode Island, requiring clinically supervised, standards-based use of algorithm-enabled tools for vision screening and prescriptions.
Keyword Tags: Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: H1: AI may not perform reserved clinical functions without licensed professional involvement.
Specific Implications: Establishes standards for remote or automated 'assessment mechanisms' used to generate eye care prescriptions or recommendations; requires clinically supervised, standards-based use of algorithm-enabled tools for vision screening. 2022 tele-optometry bill.

Policy Name/Number: H0410 – Use and Oversight of AI in State Government
Summary: Act 132 directs the development of policies and oversight for State government use of AI, including inventories, principles for trustworthy deployment, and reporting to the General Assembly. Establishes governance processes to evaluate risks and benefits of AI used by agencies.
Healthcare Implications: Vermont health agencies and partners must assess AI systems used for eligibility, licensing, and public‑health analytics under statewide policies. Vendors connecting to State systems should expect disclosure and risk‑assessment requirements.
Stakeholder Tags: Developers & Vendors
Taxonomy Rules: P2: Deploying entities must publicly disclose or file with regulators detailed information about their AI systems, policies, or inventories. | G2: Directs an agency to issue binding rules, standards, or regulatory guidance on AI. | G1: Advisory study commission or task force.
Specific Implications: Agency of Digital Services must inventory all automated decision systems used by state agencies; Division of AI proposes state code of ethics, makes policy recommendations, reports annually. | Establishes Division of AI within Agency of Digital Services as permanent oversight body. | Establishes AI Advisory Council with diverse-background members.

Policy Name/Number: AB133 – Health
Summary: Health omnibus trailer bill establishing the California Health & Human Services Data Exchange Framework (DxF) with a single Data Sharing Agreement and common Policies & Procedures for statewide health-data exchange.
Healthcare Implications: Healthcare data-infrastructure law, not AI regulation. The DxF can support future analytics or AI use by improving health-information exchange, but it does not create AI-specific governance, safety, transparency, or clinical obligations.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Payers & Purchasers; Regulators & Government; Patients & Public
Taxonomy Rules: None
Specific Implications: None

Policy Name/Number: SB21-169 – Protecting Consumers from Unfair Discrimination in Insurance Practices
Summary: Colorado’s first-in-the-nation insurance antidiscrimination law that restricts insurers’ use of external consumer data and information sources, algorithms, and predictive models, prohibiting unfair discrimination based on protected characteristics and requiring the Insurance Commissioner to adopt rules for specific lines of insurance that mandate testing, documentation, and governance of these models.
Healthcare Implications: Applies to health insurers’ use of external consumer data and algorithmic or AI-driven models for underwriting, pricing, marketing, and claims, pushing plans to implement governance and quantitative testing to show their models do not unfairly discriminate against protected groups; this can shape how AI-enabled risk scores, fraud analytics, and benefit design tools are built, validated, and monitored in Colorado’s health insurance market, with downstream implications for access, affordability, and equity in coverage.
Keyword Tags: Equity & Bias; Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Privacy & Data
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: E1: AI systems must be evaluated for discriminatory or biased outcomes; entities must take specified action if discrimination is found. | U3: AI must meet fairness, consistency, and non-discrimination standards in coverage decisions. | U4: Regulator oversight of insurer AI use (certification, reporting, audit authority). | P1: Internal procedures (impact assessments, periodic reviews, training) the deploying entity must maintain. | G2: Directs an agency to issue binding rules, standards, or regulatory guidance on AI.
Specific Implications: Insurers may not use ECDIS, algorithms, or predictive models in a way that results in unfair discrimination against protected classes; must establish risk management framework to detect unfair discrimination. | Fairness/non-discrimination standard applied to algorithmic insurance practices including utilization management and claims management. | Commissioner examination/investigation authority over insurer algorithm use; may restrict or prohibit use. | Risk management framework required; compliance reporting and quantitative testing. | Commissioner directed to promulgate sector-specific regulations (life insurance rules adopted Nov 2023; health benefit plan rules adopted Oct 2025). Predates most AI-in-healthcare bills (enacted July 2021).

Policy Name/Number: HB2154 – Patient Access to Intelligent Personal Assistants
Summary: Requires hospitals, nursing homes, and certified nursing facilities to adopt policies that allow patient access to intelligent personal assistants (e.g., voice‑activated devices) subject to privacy and safety controls. The Board of Health must amend regulations accordingly.
Healthcare Implications: Encourages patient engagement/assistive technologies in inpatient settings, with implications for Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act/privacy safeguards, device configuration, and infection‑control protocols. Facilities should document consent and technical controls when enabling AI‑enabled devices for patients.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public
Taxonomy Rules: PR1: Facilities must allow patient-provided AI assistive technologies.
Specific Implications: Hospitals, nursing homes, and certified nursing facilities must adopt policies allowing patient access to intelligent personal assistants (e.g., voice-activated devices) subject to privacy and safety controls.

Policy Name/Number: SJR71 – Artificial Intelligence Committee to Study and Advise Governor and Legislature on State Policy Issues
Summary: Creates a joint legislative commission to study advanced technology and AI and recommend policy options to the Legislature. The commission evaluates workforce, economic development, and regulatory impacts and reports findings back to lawmakers. Membership includes legislators and stakeholders.
Healthcare Implications: Not health-specific, but the commission’s scope includes workforce and automation issues that affect the healthcare labor pipeline and training needs. Findings could inform Alabama’s approach to artificial intelligence oversight in clinical and administrative settings. Outputs may shape future state legislation relevant to hospitals and health systems.
Taxonomy Rules: G1: Advisory study commission or task force.
Specific Implications: Creates joint legislative commission to study AI and recommend policy options (2019). Earliest bill in the dataset.

Policy Name/Number: H0378 – Artificial Intelligence Task Force
Summary: Creates a temporary Artificial Intelligence Task Force to investigate AI, assess benefits and risks, examine whether and how Vermont should use AI in state government, and recommend whether state regulation of AI is needed. The task force included representatives from state agencies, civil liberties, labor, academia, ethics and human rights, engineering, science, a student member, and the Vermont Medical Society, and was required to submit reports in 2019.
Healthcare Implications: Healthcare relevance is indirect and historical. The Vermont Medical Society seat gave the task force a health-sector perspective, and the review could inform later state AI governance affecting public health, licensing, Medicaid, and health data systems. The act did not create EHR data-localization requirements or AI-related EHR data-field requirements.
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Providers & Health Systems
Taxonomy Rules: G1: Advisory study commission or task force.
Specific Implications: Creates a temporary Artificial Intelligence Task Force, including a Vermont Medical Society representative, to study AI benefits and risks, state-government AI use, and whether Vermont should regulate AI, with reports due to legislative committees in 2019.

Policy Name/Number: HB2300 – Mental Health Clinical Advisory Group Algorithms (ORS 414.359)
Summary: Establishes the Mental Health Clinical Advisory Group in the Oregon Health Authority to develop evidence-based algorithms for prescription drug treatment of mental health disorders in medical assistance recipients and advise on drug coverage policies.
Healthcare Implications: Creates a formal governance structure for algorithm-based mental health treatment protocols in Medicaid, influencing how algorithmic prescribing and prior-authorization tools are designed, implemented, and updated for behavioral health care.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Providers & Health Systems; Payers & Purchasers; Regulators & Government
Taxonomy Rules: None
Specific Implications: None
